subtitle: Frog Knows Best, Mostly
location_text: The wreath at the deck's spine — every named space in vol5 visible in the four corners. The closer.
format_note: 0 ↔ XXI mirror: Fool's beginning becomes the World's closing. The deck's longest reach across a single character is John's: the diner counter at the Fool's frame returns here, dawn-lit, with the dog asleep and the wipes counted. The cycle does not end; it closes such that the next opening is already implicit.

ciphers.text: the wreath — laurel-and-vine, full circle, four corner figures (ox / lion / eagle / man) each annotated with a vol5 character name
ciphers.encoding_hint: the classical World card shows a dancing figure in a laurel wreath, with the four evangelist-figures (Tetramorph) at the corners. Modern Mythology renders the dancer as offscreen — the wreath is empty, four corners filled. OX = Dante (Emperor), LION = Faith (Strength's beast), EAGLE = Evangeline (Tower), MAN = Frank (Sun + Temperance). The four named cards are the deck's structural pillars; the dancer's absence at the center is the chapter's signature.

ciphers.text: the chapter's title — 'Frog Knows Best, Mostly' — inscribed across the bottom border
ciphers.encoding_hint: the World card's subtitle introduces the deck's ONLY explicit oracle. The Frog has been absent every prior card; arrives at XXI to render the chapter's verdict. MOSTLY — the qualification is the chapter's argument. The deck's final card is also the deck's most hedged: knowledge is partial, the closure is qualified. The Frog is the deck's quietest authority.

ciphers.text: the upper-left corner shows D'Ambrosio's diner — dawn-lit, the counter wiped clean, the dog asleep, the bindle on its hook
ciphers.encoding_hint: 0 ↔ XXI mirror: the deck's first frame returns as the deck's final corner. The Fool's 3:47 AM diner is now at dawn — the clock has moved. The dog is asleep. The bindle is hung. The counter is clean. Modern Mythology's closure: the chapter that didn't move IS the chapter that ended, because the time of day finally changed.

the three refused cards are listed in a small footer. Two held, one flipped. The deck's final card acknowledges the refusals as part of its closing — the World does not consummate the Lovers or the Judgement; the wreath closes around their face-down state. Justice's flip is itself a kind of refusal kept. The deck's relationship to its own NO is the deck's identity at closing.

the Lovers' pair lattice — six rows — accounted for at the deck's close. Three explicit confirmations (John&Faith via Fool, Quentin&Antonio via Chariot, Maya&Y via Chariot's Jimmy reveal), three implicit (handshakes, tapes, thrones — confirmed by cross-card hooks but not by name). The World's wreath closes around six pairs in three states. The deck does not consummate any of them; it accounts for all of them.

ciphers.text: the BBS header from the Fool card — now reading 'ACTIVE NODES: 64' again
ciphers.encoding_hint: Fool's banner read ACTIVE NODES: 64 at chapter 0. Chariot's wreck dropped the count to 63 at VII. The World's wreath restores the count to 64 — not because the_charioteer is back online, but because the chapter renders the gathering at the count the deck began with. Closure as numeric restoration. 64 = the full deck, the four suits at 16 each plus the Majors at 22, or whatever arithmetic the chapter is using. The number is the deck's quiet brag: we landed on the count we left from.

hotspots.notes: DORMANT: appears only after the player has examined the 3:47 AM clock on the Fool card. The upper-left corner of the World's wreath now visibly shows the same diner — but dawn-lit, the counter wiped clean, the dog asleep, the bindle on its hook. 0 ↔ XXI mirror: the deck's first frame returns as the deck's final corner. The chapter that didn't move IS the chapter that ended, because the time of day finally changed.

DORMANT: appears only after the player has read the ensemble on the Judgement card. The wreath's center, empty at first read, now visibly contains the negative space of the Judgement card's ensemble — every figure's shape as a hole in the laurel. XX → XXI: Judgement gathered the cast and refused to render the verdict; World closes the wreath around their absence. The dancer at the World's center is the gathered cast withheld. Two refusals composing one closure.

DORMANT: appears only after the Strength's rescuer-signature hotspot fires. The wreath at the bottom-centre now visibly carries a small gap in the laurel — a few leaves missing where the bottom of the closure should be unbroken. The gap is the Sinkhole. The dancer is not coming; the wreath holds the absence; the absence is the Sinkhole. The deck's structural Nexus is preserved here as a geometric fact: the closing card's closing motif has a small open mouth at its lowest point. The Frog's qualified verdicts in the closing reading take on a new register: the chapter ends; the chapter of the family ends; Alberto begins; the wreath holds; the gap remains. The gap is what Alberto cannot close. His vol6 redemption arc will include the discipline of leaving the gap visible — paying back what can be paid back, naming what can be named, and acknowledging that the gap will not become unbroken in his lifetime. The deck closes on the Nexus's structural shape: a wreath with a small dark mouth at the bottom that the painted World's iconography has been refusing to look at all along.

DORMANT: appears only after the Emperor's two-mirror-faces hotspot fires. The OX corner of the wreath (Dante's Tetramorph figure) is now visibly UNPAIRED — the corner has no matriarch figure beside the ox. The other corners (LION/Faith, EAGLE/Evangeline, MAN/Frank) each carry their natural pairing in the deck's symbology; the OX corner stands alone. The void where Dante's wife would have been a corner-paired figure is rendered as the missing register. The chapter closes around her absence as much as it closes around the Lovers' refusals.

ENTRY HOTSPOT: clicking the Frog at the wreath's center opens the deck's closing oracle. The Frog reads all 22 arcana in order with brief qualified verdicts ('mostly' as the deck's signature discipline). Click any card row to read the Frog's gloss. The reading ends with XXI THE WORLD — the deck reads the chapter the reader is in. The Frog is the only diorama-source that is not a demon (the_frog speaks for itself; integrity MOSTLY).

DORMANT: appears only after the Priestess pomegranate-hour shelf has been unlocked. The card now visibly carries a small burgundy VHS-spine label in the corner, marked "XXI — THE WORLD · 'the wreath'". Clicking it opens Elicia's relationship to this arcana as expressed in her episode for it. Elicia's relationship to closure is that she does not close. The World episode is her honest record of a circle with a gap. The wreath is a wreath of Halsey wildflowers from Plot IV; the gap is the void mother's silhouette; the slow rotation across twenty-two minutes brings the gap to dead center by the credits. The deck's last card is the deck's most reserved figure rendered as the absence the chapter contains. The Frog at XXI does not read the episode aloud, but the Frog's silence is congruent with Elicia's. The deck's closing image is the series' closing image. The series taught the deck how to end.

easter_eggs: the dancing figure at the center of the classical World card is the chapter's most explicit absence. Modern Mythology refuses to depict the dancer; the center is empty, the four corners are full, the wreath closes around what would have been the resolution.

the Frog is the deck's only oracle — absent every prior card, arrives at XXI to render the verdict. MOSTLY is the qualification: the deck's final knowledge is hedged. The Frog is the deck's quietest authority.
the four corners assign the Tetramorph to four chapters: OX = Emperor (IV), LION = Strength (VIII), EAGLE = Tower (XVI), MAN = Sun/Temperance (XIX/XIV). The structural pillars are named.
0 ↔ XXI mirror returns the Fool's diner — dawn-lit instead of 3:47 AM. The clock finally moved. Closure renders the same scene at a different hour.

ACTIVE NODES: 64 is restored. Fool opened with 64, Chariot dropped it to 63 (the_charioteer D.O.A.), World returns the count not by resurrection but by reaching the chapter count the deck began with. The number is the deck's quiet brag.

the pair lattice from the Lovers card is accounted for in the World's footer: three confirmed, three implicit. The deck does not consummate any of the six; it accounts for all of them. World accounts; Lovers refused; Judgement held. The deck's relationship to pairing is in three positions across three cards.

cross_references.fool_mirror: fool.json (0 ↔ XXI, the first scene returns at dawn)
cross_references.judgement_adjacency: judgement.json::jdg_ensemble_first (XX → XXI: gathered cast → closed wreath)
